summary: Joel Spolsky briefly notes responses to his earlier article criticizing Microsoft Passport. He observes that the only defender of Passport was a long-time Microsoft employee, suggesting that extended time within Microsoft's culture creates a perspective bubble that diverges from how the broader world views their products.
key_points: The only defense of Microsoft Passport came from a 10-year Microsoft veteran
Spolsky suggests that long tenure at Microsoft creates an insular worldview
Even personally trusted and respected colleagues can have blind spots shaped by corporate culture
money_quotes: the only person who defended Passport was a 10-year Microsoft employee — a person who I know, trust, like, and respect, but who, I'm afraid to say, has been living in Redmond too long to see things the way the rest of the world sees them.
key_insight: Working inside a large tech company for too long can create a perspective bubble that prevents you from seeing your products the way the outside world does.
